game_name: Dungeons & Dragons: Eye of the Beholder

danger_signals.visual_form: First-person rendered 3D creature sprites in dungeon view
danger_signals.meaning: Direct threat requiring combat; monster type telegraphs damage type and tactical challenge

danger_signals.visual_form: Dungeon geometry obstacles, trapped passages, environmental damage indicators
danger_signals.meaning: Spatial danger; progression blocked or character health threatened

danger_signals.visual_form: Dead bodies, bones, destroyed equipment visible in dungeon spaces
danger_signals.meaning: This area has been lethal to others; danger level communicated through environmental storytelling

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual transition from sewers → dwarf clan areas → drow clan areas → Xanathar's lair
danger_signals.meaning: Escalating threat intensity; deeper = more dangerous

safety_signals.visual_form: Character roster showing up to 6 party members with equipment and status
safety_signals.meaning: Larger party size = greater safety margin; indicates readiness for next challenge
Visual confirmation of survival capacity; full resources signal readiness

safety_signals.visual_form: Explored grid spaces with no remaining enemies or hazards
safety_signals.meaning: Secure areas for rest and regrouping

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Dwarf-specific enemy sprites; may be hostile or neutral depending on faction alignment
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dwarf clan territorial areas in mid-dungeon levels; dwarven architecture and settlements
Drow territories in deeper dungeon sections; different architectural style from dwarf areas

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: The Beholder (Xanathar) itself serves as primary faction symbol
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Beholder cult minions, summoned creatures, magical constructs
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Xanathar's lair at dungeon depths; cult-themed architecture and symbols
City entrance/sewer entrance at game start; represents external authority commissioning the adventure

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game start - threat unknown, dungeon sealed
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: City of Waterdeep accessible; sewer entrance visible but unstable
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: NPCs in city provide quest briefing; players must organize 4-character party before descent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Flooded dungeon aesthetic, water hazards, basic monster encounters, limited faction presence
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Lower enemy difficulty; exploration-focused; few NPC interactions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Clan-specific architecture becomes visible; faction members appear as NPCs and enemies
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Faction politics begin; players encounter peaceful NPCs of competing clans; alliance opportunities emerge

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Beholder-themed environment; cult imagery; powerful monster concentration
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat intensity increases; faction NPCs scarce; final threats converge visually

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Text-only ending window (DOS version) or animated sequence (Amiga version) showing return to city
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Party returns to surface; recognized as heroes by Waterdeep authority; adventure concludes

environmental_storytelling.example: Dead NPCs and their skeletal remains found in dungeons; discoverable equipment tells story of previous adventurers' fates
Visual transition from generic sewer structures to dwarven stonework to drow dark architecture communicates faction territory without exposition
Dwarf NPCs found in dwarf territories, drow in drow areas; physical presence in specific zones reinforces faction control and narrative geography
Early levels populated with basic monsters; deeper levels feature faction-specific creatures and beholder cultists; progression communicates threat escalation
Sewer entrance blocked by Xanathar-caused collapse establishes the beholder as active antagonist and creates narrative urgency

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Provide dialogue, may join party as recruitable members; signal safety in dwarf territories
Attack on sight in certain faction conflict scenarios; indicate territorial aggression
Provide dialogue, may join party; signal safety in drow territories
Attack on sight in faction conflict zones; indicate clan territory defense
Appear in encounter grid spaces; initiate combat upon proximity; no special behavioral tells
Statistically powerful; uses magic and special attacks; defeat triggers ending sequence
Static; indicate previous danger; can sometimes be resurrected as party members by player magic

notes: Eye of the Beholder uses first-person dungeon perspective (similar to Dungeon Master) to communicate world state through visual layering: surface city → sewer entry → clan territories → boss lair. Faction identity is communicated primarily through NPC appearance and location rather than explicit color/symbol systems. The game's political situation emerges through encounter structure: dwarf vs. drow clan presence in mid-dungeon creates implicit territorial conflict narrative without explicit exposition. Environmental storytelling relies on architecture shifts, corpse placement, and NPC distribution rather than decorative environmental detail. The blocky 3D rendering style limits granular visual communication, compensated by clear sprite differentiation between faction types and monster classes. Beholder as antagonist is communicated through the dungeon collapse mechanic and lair-specific environment design rather than recurring visual symbols.
